content.value:
where I show how I apply them in documentation.
To my surprise, the post got a great response — over 200 likes in just two days — and I received several private messages from people asking for more details.Instead of replying with an endless post, I decided to put together this article so I could address each question with the time and detail they deserve.
My goal:
clarify when, how, and why to use illustrations in DS documentation
, and share some brilliant examples from teams who have nailed it.Let’s get straight to the most common questions 👇
What’s the difference between a concept illustration, a technical diagram, and an illustration as a DS asset?
Not all illustrations or diagrams serve the same purpose, and understanding the difference helps avoid mixing or confusing resources.First, it’s important to be clear:
part of the DS as a system. They’re editorial resources we use in documentation, if needed.
an integral part of the system and are used throughout the product alongside components and foundations.

They accompany text, a principle, or an idea. They could be there or not, and their absence wouldn’t break anything.
An example of this can be seen in my

section with avatars of people with different profiles, inviting the viewer to reflect on how we perceive others — especially when we can’t easily define them.
I also invite you to check out my other examples on
, and more.Press enter or click to view image in full sizeAbstract concept illustrations bring life and personality to something that would otherwise be just text. They work great for:
Cards explaining principles, pillars, or best practices (or linking to other sections)

Built with geometric shapes and solid colors, without text or decorative details.
They’re useful for highlighting specific UI elements — colors, hierarchy, effects, behaviors, processes — without the distraction of the final design.In short: they’re a

that abstracts the real component to explain how it’s structured and how it behaves.
Example: showing a wireframe of a component in the header of its documentation instead of a direct Figma screenshot.Press enter or click to view image in full size
, exported directly from Figma or the design tool you use, showing the component or flow exactly as it will appear in the product.They’re used to document:
Interactive behaviors (dropdown open/closed, loading, input errors…)
Press enter or click to view image in full sizeThey’re the
— visual evidence of the real product, not a purely narrative or decorative resource.
These are an official part of the

illustrations.
For example, a “blank folder” might appear on multiple screens where there are no results, or a purchase-related sticker could indicate that no purchases were found for a search.These visual elements follow the DS foundations and are used throughout the entire product.Press enter or click to view image in full size💡

Not all DSs have a solid or well-developed illustration system. Many limit themselves to well-defined iconography, or at most, a second layer of sticker/pictogram assets.When I talk about a “system,” I mean elements that can be
(icons, “empty / no results / offline” states), not one-off illustrations used only once (like app onboarding screens that are shown at first launch and never again).

No.
The heart of a Design System is

over any type of illustration.Illustrations add visual value and context, but they don’t replace the essentials.
A DS without any illustrations can work perfectly if its components are well documented in high fidelity.
